Performance displayed represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that when shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For up-to-date fund performance, including performance current to the most recent month end, please visit our website at GuggenheimInvestments.com. Load performance reflects maximum sales charges or contingent deferred sales charges (CDSC) as applicable. A Class has a maximum sales charge of 2.25%.

The referenced fund is offered in multiple share classes. Please read the prospectus for information on fees, expenses and holding periods that may apply to each class.

Inception date of benchmark return is that of the fund’s oldest share class. The Net Expense Ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver by the Adviser through February 1, 2025, to limit the ordinary operating expenses of the fund. The Adjusted Expense Ratio (“Adjusted”) is based on the Net Expense Ratio, but excludes certain investment expenses, such as interest expense from borrowings and repurchase agreements and dividend expense from investments on short sales. Benchmark-Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond 1-3 Year Total Return Index.

Unless otherwise noted, data is as of 12.31.2024. Data subject to change. Partial year returns are cumulative. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses. Index data source: FundStation.

Performance Review

The Fund (Institutional Class) finished the fourth quarter up 0.28 percent (net), outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond 1-3 Years Index, by 0.30 percent. This brought the net YTD absolute and relative performances to 5.98 percent and 1.60 percent, respectively. Outperformance was driven largely by sector and security selection as well as the Fund’s carry advantage over the benchmark.

Duration detracted from absolute returns as interest rates rose following stronger-than-expected economic data and in response to the U.S. election results.

The Fund benefitted from an overweight allocation to structured credit as the sector broadly outperformed other fixed-income sectors such as corporate credit due to more pronounced spread tightening.

Strategy and Positioning

The Fund continued to position itself roughly at an average risk level relative to its historical range, reflective of the offsetting effects of strong credit fundamentals coupled with tight valuations. Positioning favors higher credit quality forms of carry, shorter duration instruments, such as senior tranches within non-Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), esoteric asset-backed securities (ABS), and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs). Securitized credit is the largest allocation within the Fund, representing approximately 40 percent. The allocation is well- diversified and consists largely of CLOs as well as various commercial ABS and RMBS subsectors. Yields across these sectors are at notably attractive levels versus corporate credit.

The Fund maintained a slight duration overweight versus the benchmark. The Agency RMBS allocation increased by 2 percent during the quarter, with exposure ending at approximately 17 percent of the Fund. The allocation is concentrated in near-production pools which offer the highest carry profile and the widest nominal spreads and present the most direct route within the coupon stack to monetize the current state of heightened interest rate volatility.

The Fund marginally decreased its exposure to investment-grade corporates to an approximate 25 percent allocation. While index level spreads are compressed, an active primary market offered some opportunities to add marginally to the sector at reasonable pricing concessions.

The Fund maintains an above-average allocation to defensive sectors, such as Treasurys and enhanced cash investments, to serve as dry powder and leave the Fund well-positioned to take advantage of any potential selloffs in risk assets.

QTD Contributors / Detractors

Carry contributed 1.4 percent to absolute return and over 0.1 percent to relative return. Duration detracted by 1.4 percent on an absolute basis and 0.1 percent on a relative basis. Spread tightening across structured credit contributed on an absolute and relative basis. IG corporates’ spread tightening contributed on an absolute and relative basis.

Economic and Market Review

  • The U.S. economy has good momentum heading into 2025, but the policy outlook from Washington elevates uncertainty.
  • Recent economic data have been solid, with fourth quarter real gross domestic product (GDP) on track for about 2 percent annualized growth. The outlook for consumer spending remains positive, supported by healthy growth in inflation-adjusted labor income and a wealth effect driven by rising asset prices.
  • Financial conditions have also turned more supportive as credit growth is reaccelerating and optimism about artificial intelligence induces a positive outlook for capex. Disinflationary progress has stalled a bit in recent months, but fundamentals point to a further slowdown in inflation as wage pressures and housing inflation ease further.
  • With a new presidential administration, investors want to know how the evolving policy landscape will alter the outlook. In the immediate future, we expect a boost to both consumer and business sentiment, aided by expectations of deregulation and further tax cuts. Post-election surveys have already shown increased optimism about the outlook, which could support consumption, investment, and hiring in coming months.
  • Looking beyond the immediate sentiment boost, the outlook becomes more uncertain and depends on the ultimate policy mix of the new administration. Extension of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) would prevent a fiscal drag, but we see limited scope for new tax cuts as the fiscal backdrop has worsened. Broad tariffs could weigh on growth and push up prices, though we expect more targeted tariffs will ultimately be used.
  • All together, we see moderate growth in the U.S. economy in 2025 as these policy shifts play out. Economic fundamentals remain solid, with strong household and corporate balance sheets. The Federal Reserve (Fed) will likely ease policy further toward a neutral setting, but tariffs could slow the pace of rate cuts by interrupting the disinflationary trend. The U.S. economy should remain a global outperformer, though we expect continued bifurcation across sectors, particularly as new policies begin to have an impact.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Please see page 2 for additional performance information.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Carry: The difference between the cost of financing an asset and the interest received on that asset. Dry powder: highly liquid assets, such as cash or money market instruments, that can be invested when more attractive investment opportunities arise. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors. In general, the value of a fixed-income security falls when interest rates rise and rises when interest rates fall. Longer term bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes and subject to greater volatility than those with shorter maturities. During periods of declining rates, the interest rates on floating rate securities generally reset downward and their value is unlikely to rise to the same extent as comparable fixed rate securities.

Investors in asset-backed securities, including collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), generally receive payments that are part interest and part return of principal. These payments may vary based on the rate loans are repaid. Some asset-backed securities may have structures that make their reaction to interest rates and other factors difficult to predict, making their prices volatile and they are subject to liquidity and valuation risk.

High yield and unrated debt securities are at a greater risk of default than investment grade bonds and may be less liquid, which may increase volatility.

When market conditions are deemed appropriate, the fund will leverage to the full extent permitted by its investment policies and restrictions and applicable law. Leveraging will exaggerate the effect on net asset value of any increase or decrease in the market value of the fund’s portfolio.

The fund may invest in derivative instruments, which may be more volatile and less liquid, increasing the risk of loss when compared to traditional securities. Certain of the derivative instruments are also subject to the risks of counterparty default and adverse tax treatment.

Instruments and strategies (such as borrowing transactions and reverse repurchase agreements) may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments.

Investments in reverse repurchase agreements expose the fund to the many of the same risks as investments in derivatives. The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle.

The fund’s investments in foreign securities carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political, or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks).

Investments in syndicated bank loans generally offer a floating interest rate and involve special types of risks.

The fund’s investments in municipal securities can be affected by events that affect the municipal bond market.

The fund’s investments in real estate securities subject the fund to the same risks as direct investments in real estate, which is particularly sensitive to economic downturns.

The fund’s investments in restricted securities may involve financial and liquidity risk.

You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares.

It is important to note that the fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. government. Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Index Definition. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond 1–3 Year Total Return Index measures the performance of publicly issued investment grade corporate, U.S. Treasury and government agency securities with remaining maturities of one to three years.

This material is not intended as a recommendation or as investment advice of any kind, including in connection with rollovers, transfers, and distributions. Such material is not provided in a fiduciary capacity, may not be relied upon for or in connection with the making of investment decisions, and does not constitute a solicitation of an offer to buy or sell securities. All content has been provided for informational or educational purposes only and is not intended to be and should not be construed as legal or tax advice and/or a legal opinion. Always consult a financial, tax, and/or legal professional regarding your specific situation.

Read the fund’s prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) carefully before investing. It contains the fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information, which should be considered carefully before investing. Obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) at GuggenheimInvestments.com.

The referenced fund is distributed by Guggenheim Funds Distributors, LLC. Guggenheim Investments represents the investment management businesses of Guggenheim Partners, LLC (“Guggenheim”), which includes Guggenheim Partners Investment Management (“GPIM”), the investment advisor to the referenced fund. Guggenheim Funds Distributors, LLC, is affiliated with Guggenheim and GPIM.

data.title: Weighing The Hammer
data.description:
Another spasmodic séance gripped Wall Street today as investors squinted over the tariff tarot cards. Stocks staged a 3% levitation act this morning before fading to the close, landing close enough to a bear market to count the fillings in its teeth.

Level Change 4/8/25 (%)

-0.8 Dow

-2.2 Nasdaq

-2.0 Nasdaq 100

-1.6 S&P 500

-2.2 S&P 400

-3.0 S&P 600

Even volatility has been volatile. The VIX popped to 60 yesterday, slipped under 40 this morning in a bit of false calm, then lunged back to 52 by the close.

“Classic bear market behavior,” say analysts, between sips of antacid. Indeed. Since February 19, the S&P 500 is down 19%, while the Nasdaq, ever the overachiever, has shed 24%.

It’s all about tariffs, naturally. Investors are still trying to get their arms around Trump’s trade war, but keep ending up in a bear hug with economic slowdown and inflation.

Stocks perked up this morning on signs that Team Trump may finally be catching on: in the saber-rattling business, it helps to look like you’re willing to sheath one now and then. Both Treasury Secretary Bessent and National Economic Council head Hassett made conciliatory noises, name-dropping Japan and South Korea as potential partners in dialogue.

Trump said China “wants to make a deal.” He’s waiting by the phone - and might be there a while. China’s Commerce Ministry said it would “fight to the end.”

Investors gulped. The end of what?

Then it all came unglued around 10:30, when the White House confirmed a 104% tariff on China will take effect tomorrow, punishment for failing to remove its retaliation against US retaliatory tariffs.

The 104% figure struck onlookers as rather steep, more like a ransom demand than a tariff. “I’ve seen bribes smaller than that,” one trader was overheard telling a colleague clutching a stress ball.

In testimony to the Senate finance committee, US Trade Rep Jamieson Greer defended the administration’s sweeping tariffs. He said the US must “move away from an economy that’s based solely on the financial sector and government spending” to one that produces “real goods and services.”

Ron Wyden (D-OR) countered that the “aimless” and “chaotic” tariff rollout took the US economy “from the envy of the world to a laughingstock in less time than it took to finish March Madness.” Thom Tillis (R-NC) asked, “Whose throat do I get to choke if this proves to be wrong?”

Greer assured senators that Trump’s tariffs are already bearing negotiation fruit, adding that, no, there won’t be any exemptions or exclusions. These are tariffs for all seasons.

Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said the tariffs are “way bigger” than anything the Fed modeled. His recent talks with business people found anxiety rising faster than a teenager’s temperature when the wi-fi goes out: “The anxiety is this is just going to take us back to a thing we spent the last five years desperately trying to get away from.” No, not Trump - inflation.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly repeated the one about policy being in a good place for the Fed to “tread slowly,” but she worries about inflation.

Sentiment at the National Federation of Independent Business has been way ahead of the curve - unfortunately, the curve of despondence. It fell for the third straight month in March, this time by 3.3 points to 97.4, just below the 51-year average of 98.

The release noted that small businesses have reined in sales growth expectations, thanks to the fog surrounding whatever it is Washington currently calls policy implementation.

Don’t give up hope just yet. Recent jumpiness, plus the S&P’s double-dip into bear market territory and back out, suggests investors are ready to stampede back into stocks on any good trade news.

Keep your fingers crossed for some.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: URNM: Uranium Equities Extremely Out Of Favor, Provides A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Overview & Performance

The Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:URNM) is a pure-play uranium equities ETF. This ETF was launched in 2019 as the North Shore Global Uranium Mining ETF and was in 2022 acquired by Sprott and then renamed to the Sprott Uranium Miners ETF.

The ETF has 36 holdings and the total assets under management ("AUM") is around $1.1B, The three largest holdings Cameco (CCJ), Kazatomprom, and the Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (OTCPK:SRUUF) account for about 44% of its holdings. URNM is reasonably well diversified, but it is natural that the largest uranium equities with better liquidity are such a large part of the ETF, to support the investment flows that can be substantial at times.

URNM has over the last couple of years seen healthy net inflows, where the share count has increased by 38% over the last 2 years. The AUM has almost been cut in half compared to the peak at the beginning of last year, due to poor recent performance and an extremely depressed sentiment lately.

The spot price of uranium has been roughly flat over the last three years and peaked early last year. The term price of uranium has had more of a steady grind higher to now $80/lb, which equates to a 63% increase over the last 3 years. However, during this time, the Sprott Uranium Miners ETF has managed to decline 30%.

Part of this recent weakness is no doubt due to the weaker spot price, even if most of the uranium is traded on term contracts. However, the poor stock market performance in 2025 has also fueled the decline in uranium equities. This has in my view taken uranium equities in general and URNM down to a level not supported by fundamentals, which is why I rate URNM as a strong buy at this level.

Reasoning on Recent Tariffs

The U.S. tariffs are currently dominating the headlines and the stock markets, which is natural given that the impact to many companies, the economy, and the overall market will be substantial. However, given that uranium looks to be exempt from tariffs, the fundamental impact for URNM will be minimal. Even in a scenario with more broad-based tariffs on uranium, we might see a slight premium on U.S. and possibly Canadian uranium, but the impact would otherwise be relatively limited on uranium equities.

With that said, uranium equities have over the last few years been sensitive to overall stock market performance, especially to the downside. So, if uranium equities weren't that depressed going into 2025, the weak performance in URNM might have made more sense. However, right now, we look to be in a liquidation phase of this re-alignment, where sentiment is driving performance more than fundamentals.

I primarily focus on the fundamentals, which have never been better for nuclear and uranium. Most nuclear reactors under construction are today in Asia, but we have also seen a lot of commitments from countries around the world, which will turn into new constructions over the coming years. At the same time, relatively few nuclear reactors are closed, and life extensions up to 80+ years are now relatively common.

As mentioned earlier, while the uranium spot market might be a decent sentiment indicator, it is not where most of the uranium is traded. That is done in the term market, where the price has been relatively strong, despite lackluster volumes.

We did see term volumes pick up in 2023, but volumes have been lower since then. It is natural that utilities are focused on other things, which might be more impacted by the recent tariff turbulence. However, uranium contracting has been well below replacement level over the last decade, which can't continue forever. Once it picks up, I expect the term price will strengthen further, which will be difficult for uranium equities to deny.

Once the most acute turbulence settles from tariffs, I expect fundamentals will once again regain the upper hand for uranium equities. The risk of a recession for both the U.S. and many other countries has increased lately.

However, the demand for uranium doesn't change much even during a global recession, so I wouldn't be overly concerned about that. The below chart was cropped just before the effects on nuclear power following Fukushima. You can, in 2008-2010, barely see any impact on nuclear electricity generation and consequently uranium demand.

Conclusion

I have been invested in uranium equities since 2018, and we have since then seen a massive nuclear renaissance, which was difficult to imagine back then. Countries like Japan, the UK, France, South Korea, and Sweden have all made U-turns from planning to phase out nuclear to now actively be looking at new builds.

If we use a longer-term view, which I like to do. I do think it is fair to say we are still in a bull market for the uranium term price that started in 2020, even if that doesn't apply to uranium equities, which have retraced a lot lately.

The relatively strong performance in URNM since 2020 has been punctuated with drawdowns along the way, as we saw in 2022 and into the first half of 2023. The drawdown in URNM from May last year is now up to 51%, which is a massive disconnect when we consider that the term price has declined 1.8% from its recent peak in November last year.

I haven't played the uranium market perfectly, but I have done relatively well since 2020. That is from having been conservative going into 2022 and more aggressive during softer periods. I am once again relatively aggressive in uranium equities, as I consider this an excellent buying opportunity for anyone with a multi-year perspective.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AVSC: Avantis Investors' Small Cap ETF Is Solid But Too Diversified
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Avantis US Small Cap Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:AVSC) has a low 0.25% expense ratio and an average market cap of only $1.44 billion, virtually guaranteeing it to be a nice complement to other funds in your portfolio. However, like most other core small-cap ETFs, AVSC is too diversified and doesn't do a good enough job of weeding out non-profitable stocks. As a solution, I ask readers to consider the Hartford Multifactor Small Cap ETF (ROSC), which outpaces AVSC on most fundamental metrics and has outperformed by over 7% since AVSC's inception three years ago. After presenting the fundamental differences between these two and three others, I hope you'll agree that AVSC is solid but not optimal, and as always, I look forward to your questions and comments afterward.

AVSC Overview

Strategy Discussion

According to its fact sheet, AVSC "invests in a diverse group of U.S. small-cap companies, taking into consideration valuation, profitability and levels of investment when selecting and weighting securities." Core small-cap exposure is its purpose - AVSC is not a "themed" fund where it only highlights one or two factors like value or momentum. Instead, it invests in a broad basket of nearly 1,000 stocks and slightly tilts each stock's weight to meet its desired objectives. Plus, as an actively managed fund, AVSC can make changes as necessary, and if we use the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) or the iShares Russell 2000 Value ETF (IWN) as benchmarks, I'd say AVSC has been a relative success so far, outperforming by about 3-4% since its launch on January 11, 2022.

AVSC's prospectus document adds some color to how it selects stocks. I've summarized a few key points below for reference:

1. The fund emphasizes companies with small market caps and securities with higher profitability and value characteristics.

2. Portfolio managers use historical and forecasted company financials in their assessment, including shares outstanding, book value, operating cash flow, and accruals.

3. Value is primarily defined as a company's adjusted book/price ratio, while profitability means adjusted cash from operations as a percentage of a company's book value.

4. A company's market cap is used to determine a baseline weight, and then managers overweight or underweight based on the fundamental features described above.

I calculated a 44% correlation between AVSC's current components' weights and their market caps, indicating that there's quite a bit of active weighting happening here. I think it's a good feature because there are so many non-profitable stocks in the small-cap universe that you don't want in your portfolio. Using IWN as an example, they comprise about 20% of the fund, and filtering those out is a quick and easy way to improve returns.

Performance Snapshot vs. Peer Group

AVSC's $1.44 billion weighted average market cap is one of the smallest you'll find in the ETF universe. In fact, I could only find four diversified funds with a smaller size, and two of them are micro-cap funds (FDM and IWC). For that reason, finding appropriate peers was a bit challenging, but I found four that seemed to fit the bill, all with market caps around $2 billion or less.

  1. WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Earnings Fund ETF (EES)
  2. iShares US Small Cap Value Factor ETF (SVAL)
  3. Invesco S&P SmallCap Value with Momentum ETF (XSVM)
  4. Hartford Multifactor Small Cap ETF (ROSC)

As shown, AVSC's 0.25% expense ratio is quite competitive, and it's the most popular, with $1.27 billion in assets under management. XSVM is next at $571 million, $540 million of which occurred within the last five years after its Index change on June 21, 2019. EES and SVAL have also crossed the $100 million threshold, while ROSC continues to struggle, with just $29 million in assets under management. It's a shame, too, because its 1.31% decline since AVSC's launch was easily the best in this peer group.

One interesting finding is that while AVSC had similar total volatility (standard deviation) as its peers, ROSC's 21.83% annualized figure was noticeably lower, as is its current 20.85% drawdown, which is almost 5% better than AVSC's. The performance statistics below are from January 2023 onwards.

AVSC Analysis

Sector Allocations

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between AVSC, EES, SVAL, XSVM, and ROSC. In terms of diversification, I think EES and ROSC are good comparators, while SVAL and XSVM are "specialty" options to consider that focus on the value and value/momentum factors, respectively.

Regional banks are prevalent in small-cap ETFs. AVSC holds 171, which comprises 19.46% of the fund, and exposure ranges from 12.05% in ROSC to 44.30% in SVAL, suggesting that they're bound to show up in value-oriented funds. However, high exposure is usually not helpful. To illustrate, consider the following active returns for the SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE) compared to the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR).

When KRE outperforms, the active returns are typically small in the low-single-digits. However, KRE really struggled in 2007, 2009, 2020, and most recently in 2023, lagging behind by 23.65%. Therefore, I suggest carefully monitoring exposure to this industry. If you're looking to avoid it completely, an easy way is with the ALPS O'Shares U.S. Small-Cap Quality Dividend ETF (OUSM). Its ROA screen for stocks in the Financials sector consistently eliminates them all, as Regional Banks typically have ROA's of only about 1% or less.

Fundamentals By Sub-Industry

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for AVSC's top 25 sub-industries, which total 58.35% of the portfolio. This concentration level is similar to EES and ROSC, which is why they're all potential "core" holdings in your portfolio. Meanwhile, SVAL and XSVM are much more concentrated and probably work best as satellite holdings.

Here are four additional observations to consider:

AVSC: Above-Average Growth Potential

One reason to invest in AVSC is that its selections have above-average growth potential, evidenced by the portfolio's one-year estimated sales and earnings growth rates of 5.62% and 9.91%. They're about 2-3% better than the peer group average, and when scrolling through the list, I see several with double-digit earnings growth estimates like Biotechnology (11.17%), Application Software (10.00%), and Property & Casualty Insurance (13.25%). Energy is also a potential opportunity, especially since energy prices usually accompany high inflation and there is growing concern that President Trump's tariffs, if not reversed, will inevitably lead to higher prices across the board. However, that risk is not specific to AVSC. Its 6.11% allocation to this sector is relatively small and only 1.18% above the peer group average.

AVSC: Below-Average Value Features

AVSC trades at 10.87x forward earnings, which is about 1.05 points above the peer group average. While its 6.23x price/trailing cash flow ratio is attractive, I also noted how AVSC is priced high on other valuation ratios, including Avantis' preferred P/B ratio. I've summarized them all below for your reference, and also included a sector-adjusted value score that I derived from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades. The purpose of this score is to reduce sector biases and reveal XSVM as the most attractive at 6.44/10, with ROSC not far behind at 6.08/10.

AVSC: Quality Features Disappoint Due To High Diversification

So far, I've established that AVSC has good exposure to the size and growth factors but below-average exposure to the value factor. I was hoping quality would stand out, given how that's one of the main objectives, but unfortunately, it doesn't look that way. AVSC's weighted average 1.30% and 6.08% ROA and ROE figures were the lowest in the peer group, and its 5.33/10 sector-adjusted profit score is the second worst and #25/34 in the small-cap value category, according to my end-of-month calculations.

I initially thought AVSC's 19.46% allocation to Regional Banks was the culprit, but that's not the case, as the other four ETFs have allocations that average 24.00%. Instead, I found there to be too many unprofitable stocks in the fund. I counted 363/1089, which comprise 20.42% of the fund, compared to 22/309 (5.51%) for ROSC. Therefore, I believe AVSC is simply too overdiversified. As ROSC demonstrates, it's possible to achieve similar diversification at the sub-industry level with only about one-third the number of holdings, and I think this is the direction small-cap fund managers need to take. Not only does reducing exposure to non-profitable stocks improve quality, but it also reduces risk and leads to better returns in market drawdowns.

Investment Recommendation

AVSC is a solid core small-cap fund with a reasonable 0.25% expense ratio and $1.27 billion in assets under management. It aims to increase exposure to smaller companies with attractive value and quality features, but sadly, I found AVSC's value and profitability statistics underwhelming. I attribute this mainly to extreme diversification at the company level, and I would prefer Avantis' managers to focus more on the portfolio's industry diversification while weeding out more unprofitable stocks, which comprise 20% of the fund. As a result, I believe the most appropriate rating for AVSC is a "hold," and I look forward to answering any questions you might have in the comments below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: NFIB Small Business Survey: Sales Growth Expectations Scaled Back
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index dropped for a third straight month, falling to 97.4 in March. The latest reading was below the 98.9 forecast and pushes the index below the historical average for the first time since October. The index is at the 38th percentile of the series.

Key Takeaways from March's report:

  • The net percent of small business owners expecting higher sales volume for a third consecutive month after surging from recession levels after the election.
  • Labor quality remains the top issue for small businesses, followed by taxes (highest level since November 2021), and then inflation.
  • The net percent of small business owners expecting better business conditions fell for a third straight month, experiencing its largest monthly decline since December 2020.
  • The net percent of owners raising selling prices experienced its largest monthly decline since December 2022 but remains historically high.
  • Loans were harder to get and the number of owners borrowing on a regular basis increased from February.

The first chart below tracks the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index since 1986, with a baseline level of 100. Notice the sharp declines in sentiment during the Great Financial Crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic. In contrast, the index showed relative resilience during the 2000-2003 Tech Bubble collapse.

Following the Great Recession, small business sentiment remained weak for an extended period, a pattern that closely resembled the past two years of pandemic-related disruptions and high inflation. Another interesting pattern to the index was the nearly identical jumps following the 2016 and 2024 elections.

The average monthly change in the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is 1.4 points. To filter out short-term fluctuations, the chart below presents a 3-month moving average alongside the individual monthly values, represented by dots.

NFIB Small Business Survey Components

The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is composed of 10 components. In March, two components increased, seven declined, and one was unchanged.

Business Optimism and Consumer Attitudes

The next few charts are overlays of the Business Optimism Index with two of the main measures of consumer attitudes: Conference Board Consumer Confidence and University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index.

The Consumer Confidence Index is influenced by employment and labor market conditions from the worker's perspective, whereas the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on employment conditions from the business perspective. (For more information on how these indexes measure up against each other, check out our monthly update Two Measures of Consumer Attitudes: MCSI vs. CCI).

In our first chart comparing the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index with the Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index, we can see that the consumer measure is the more volatile of the two. Therefore, it is plotted on a separate axis to give a better comparison of the two series from the common baseline of 100.

Next, we compare the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index with the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. Again, we've plotted each index on a separate axis, however in this chart, we can see that the business measure is more volatile of the two.

Despite the volatility though, we can see that these two measures of mood (business and consumer) have been highly correlated, falling and rising together for the most part. A decline in Small Business Sentiment was a long leading indicator for the first two recessions of the century, but clearly not for the Covid-19 recession.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SI-BONE, Inc.: Small-cap Developer Of Medical Implants And A Steady Revenue Grower
data.description:
SI-Bone (NASDAQ:SIBN) develops implants for sacroiliac joint dysfunction, pelvic fixation and pelvic fractures. After introducing their original iFuse implant system back in 2009, the company has steadily expanded its portfolio of proprietary titanium implants and the tools required to place them, along with a suite of implantable bone products. Over the years, they’ve rolled out a series of innovations—from the 3D-printed iFuse-3D in 2017, to the screw-based iFuse TORQ in 2021, and then iFuse Bedrock Granite, designed for spinal stability, in 2022. Most recently, 2024 saw the debut of two new additions: iFuse INTRA and the trauma-focused iFuse TORQ TNT. These technologies benefit if they are FDA-approved for treating sacroiliac joint dysfunction, pelvic trauma, and adult spinal deformity.

The company primarily uses a direct sales force to market their products in the US, but also relies on third parties. Internationally, they use both these channels as well as resellers. Over the years, they have had their products used in over 115,000 procedures by over 4,300 physicians in the United States and 38 other countries. The company estimates that their “total addressable market in the United States exceeds $3.0 billion.”

The problem

The sacroiliac joint sits where the spine meets the pelvis. It is a relatively small joint in terms of motion, but large in surface area and undertakes a massively important function, to transfer the entire body weight through the pelvic area to the legs, absorb shocks and so on. The joint only allows for a small degree of rotation and translation, making it a very stable joint held together by a number of big ligaments.

Over time, or due to trauma, surgery, pregnancy, and arthritis, these ligaments can become weak, making the joint unstable, pinching on nerves and causing back pain. The SI joint shares nerve supply with the lower lumbar spine and hip, so pain can radiate in a fashion similar to lumbar disc issues or hip problems—making it difficult to diagnose.

The solutions

Depending on the severity and duration of pain, various measures are taken. Pain relievers are universally used symptomatically. Anti-inflammatory autoimmune system targeting biologics like the IL-17 targeting cosentyx and the TNF targeting humira are used to treat any underlying inflammation. Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) are also used to treat any underlying inflammation. These could include various JAK inhibitors like tofacitinib (Xeljanz) and upadacitinib (Rinvoq).

Other options could be steroid injections into the joint, or better yet, injecting “prolotherapy solution, platelet-rich plasma, or bone marrow concentrate” which can help make the ligaments healthier. How does that work?

For most patients, it means getting a precise image-guided injection of their own platelet-rich plasma (PRP) into the ligaments and joint. Prolotherapy also works as well. If those approaches don’t work, then getting your own bone marrow concentrate (BMC or a same-day stem cell procedure) injected usually gets it done.

How could these regenerative injection procedures work? By promoting the damaged ligaments to beef up and tighten. In addition, both of those procedures are much less invasive than an iFuse implant and far less costly.

Interestingly, on the Mayo Clinic website, they say this about joint fusion:

Although surgery is rarely used to treat sacroiliitis, fusing the two bones together with metal hardware can sometimes relieve sacroiliitis pain.

SIBN’s solution

When these conservative solutions do not work, doctors may prescribe one of the iFuse devices sold by SIBN. It consists of porous titanium implants with a triangular cross-section that are fused into the joint through a minimally invasive surgery, according to the British Health Board. In a procedure that takes less than an hour, these implants are placed inside the joint, allowing for more stability. The SI joint does not want to move, so the idea is to help it not to move at all by fusing it together.

Interestingly, there are both positive reports and negative reports on the procedure. First, the positive:

The difference in patient-reported outcomes between conservative management and surgical management is demonstrated in a randomized controlled trial that included 52 subjects who underwent either unilateral or bilateral minimally invasive sacroiliac arthrodesis using SI-Bone triangular titanium implants, and 51 subjects who received conservative medical management, which included physical therapy sessions for 6 months (2 subjects received additional sacroiliac corticosteroid injections and 1 subject received injections plus radiofrequency ablation).44 The self-rated results demonstrated significant low back pain improvement at 6 months and 24 months in the surgical group compared to the conservative management group.

However, the negative is far from reassuring. A good summary of everything can be found on this website.

SI-BONE’s clinical trial portfolio includes 9 long-term studies. They all aim to demonstrate the safety, effectiveness, and durability of SI joint fusion with the iFuse implant system. Across pivotal trials like INSITE, iMIA, and SIFI, patients experienced improvements in pain, disability, and quality of life versus non-surgical care. For example, long-term follow-up from LOIS confirmed sustained benefit at five years, with high fusion rates and no device-related complications.

Among competitors, Globus Medical makes the SI-LOK™ Sacroiliac Joint Fusion screws. Medtronic develops the Rialto SI Fusion System. However, SIBN says “these competitors sell products which we believe lack the features, evidence and advantages of [their] implants.”

Revenue

Despite some physicians (like the one cited above) not preferring the procedure, it is widely used, or rather, used as often as necessary, with comparatively positive results. That is reflected in SIBN’s revenue figures.

In Q4 2024, global revenue reached $49 million, up approximately 26% year over year, with US sales contributing $46.9 million—representing a 28% increase. Gross margins remained healthy at 79%, while the net loss narrowed significantly to $4.5 million, a 59% improvement. Importantly, adjusted EBITDA turned positive at $1.9 million, which is a major milestone.

Coming to the full fiscal year 2024, revenue climbed to $167.2 million, a 20% increase, driven almost entirely by the U.S. market, which grew 21% to $158.4 million. The business maintained strong gross margins at 79%, while the annual net loss improved by nearly 30%, standing at $30.9 million. Adjusted EBITDA also saw a dramatic 71% improvement, narrowing to a loss of $5.1 million.

In 2024, 1,400 active U.S. physicians used the company’s products —a 23% year-over-year increase. Notably, it secured Transitional Pass-Through (TPT) payment status for Granite procedures beginning in 2025, a milestone that should expand outpatient reimbursement access. Also, the company received its third FDA Breakthrough Device Designation.

Guidance for 2025 is optimistic, with expected revenue growth of another 16–17%, reaching up to $195.5 million, with gross margin slightly lower in the 77–78% range. Expense growth is expected to be held to about 9%, and management anticipates turning in positive adjusted EBITDA for both the second half and the full year.

Financials

SIBN has a market cap of $571mn and a cash balance of $150mn. R&D and G&A expenses were $3.9mn and $40.4mn respectively. Their CFO for the quarter was $1.2mn, while capex was ($2.3mn), so their FCF was -$1.1mn, which gives them a cash runway of 136 quarters, i.e. nearly an indefinite cash runway.

Risks

SIBN’s products have seen very strong uptake. I was a little concerned by the reports of device defects, so I looked up FDA device reports here. There are over 500 reports, however, if you look closely, you see this:

Reports in:

2025 - 1

2024 - 4

2023 - 3

2022 - 16

2021 - 151

2020 - 143

2019 - 165

And so on. You should get the idea. The company is getting better at what it does - that is the most sanguine explanation. Patients are reporting fewer problems - which, in this age of social media and net savvy people, means there are fewer problems.

Bottom Line

SIBN is an interesting implant maker with strong revenue growth. The stock stays somewhat rangebound, between $10 and $20 mostly, except for the occasional spurt. I will wait for a price closer to $10 before I consider an investment.

data.title: Diamondback Energy Has A Major Power Play At Hand
data.description:
Diamondback Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:FANG) is positioning itself to realize significant growth as a result of the increasing natural gas demand driven by the growing large-scale data center footprint that is expected to be supported by gas-fired power plants. Diamondback should benefit in two ways from this phenomenon: directly as a result of gas offtake by gas-fired facilities, and indirectly from broader domestic demand growth. The growing demand may support stronger gas prices in a time of a relatively flat oil market, which is expected to remain in the low-to-mid-$60/bbl range going forward.

Given the strong growth opportunities at hand, I am recommending FANG shares with a BUY rating and price target of $238/share at 7.66x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Diamondback Energy Operations

Diamondback's total daily volumes grew to 883Mboe/d, up 91% when compared to the previous year's quarter. This was the result of the closed merger with Endeavor Energy Resources in September 2024. Diamondback is relatively well hedged for Q1 2025 with over half of production being backed by derivatives with a price floor in the range of $55.84-60. Given the price of oil throughout the first quarter of 2025, the firm may realize a loss on its derivative positions while realizing stronger oil revenues.

Looking at the strip prices, WTI (CL1:COM) is expected to be in the range of $59.5-61/bbl throughout the duration of eFY25, potentially providing Diamondback with challenging comps when compared to FY 2024.

Natural gas has the potential of providing some upside risk to Diamondback in eFY25, as prices may continue on their upward trajectory as a result of broader macroeconomic factors. As outlined in my recent report covering Golar LNG (GLNG), the EU has fallen into a significant deficit in its gas reserves at 34% of capacity. With Russia shifting in all exports to the EU, this may mean that the EU will need to import significantly more LNG to bring its gas reserve up to an appropriate level to sustain the Bloc for the winter of 2025.

Domestically, natural gas demand is expected to increase in the coming years as natural gas-fired power plants are constructed to support the growing large-scale data center footprint. Though there was a recent setback with news that Microsoft (MSFT) canceled certain data center leases, I suspect that this was a one-off event that does not signal a slowdown across the rest of the digital economy. One interesting factor to consider is Oracle's (ORCL) project Stargate facilities being constructed in Abilene, Texas, with the target to commence operations by the end of 2026. East Daley estimates that 0.66Bcf/d of gas capacity will be traveling to the Texas city to support the 5GW of power demand for data center infrastructure. This can be a major offtake for gas flowing from the Permian Basin and potentially support elevated gas prices, especially as more gas-fired power plants are constructed through the end of the decade.

Diamondback is going the route of many producers in the Permian Basin for gas offtake. The firm is currently in discussions with a large independent power producer to offtake Diamondback-produced natural gas for in-basin, behind-the-meter power production in support of the growing data center footprint. Unlike Chevron (CVX), who is potentially seeking to own the entire gas-to-power supply chain, Diamondback will partner with a third-party power producer with the intent to also utilize a small proportion of the gas power for in-basin oilfield operations. I believe this could be accretive to Diamondback's operations, as the firm will have a known customer with the potential for a known price or premium over the spot price.

Diamondback Energy M&A

Diamondback executed a number of significant transactions at the beginning of 2025. This includes the close of the Double Eagle transaction and the drop-down transaction of Viper Energy.

Double Eagle was acquired for $3b in cash and 6.9mm shares at 5.2x 2025 EBITDA, closing on April 1, 2025. The acquisition brought Diamondback 40,000 net acres in the core of the Midland Basin, with an estimated production run-rate of 27Mbbl/d. Part of the agreement was to accelerate the development of a portion of Diamondback's non-core southern Midland assets in order to drive stronger free cash flow in 2026. In turn, Diamondback will sell $1.5b of non-core assets to pay down debt in order to maintain its strong balance sheet.

Diamondback also closed its $26b acquisition of Endeavor Energy in September 2024. This merger brought total production up to 816Mboe/d across its 838,000 net acres. Management anticipates total synergies to sum to $550mm, primarily the result of reduced operating costs.

Management anticipates that Diamondback will have the flexibility to sell acreage outside of their operated assets. In addition to this, the firm is considering selling some equity method investments.

The Viper Energy deal is expected to close in Q2 2025 for a value of $4.45b. This transaction is for a definitive purchase agreement with Viper to sell certain mineral and royalty interests from subsidiaries of Diamondback for $1b in cash and 69.9mm units of Viper's operating subsidiary.

Diamondback Energy Financial Position

For Q1 2025, I'm forecasting Diamondback to generate $3.9b in net revenue, with a diluted EPS of $3.76/share. This is supported by strong oil and gas prices throughout Q1 2025 paired with some cost reductions following the close of the Endeavor acquisition.

Management remains focused on improving operational efficiency, bringing forth a new metric to provide further insight into their success. The metric provides the WTI price needed for the current fiscal year to achieve equivalent adjusted free cash flow generation when compared to the previous fiscal year's oil price. For example, this year's WTI price will need to average $67/bbl in order to match the FY 2024 adjusted free cash flow when WTI was priced at $76/bbl. I believe that this metric provides significant insights into the inner operations of Diamondback and how the firm is becoming more capital-efficient.

The capital outlay for eFY25 sums to $3.6-4b. As part of the firm's drive to improve drilling efficiency, management anticipates reducing the capital costs per well down to $2.2-2.4/well, saving $200mm in eFY25.

Diamondback ended Q4 2024 with $161mm in cash & equivalent and $13b in total debt on the balance sheet, for a leverage ratio of 1.78x net debt/aEBITDA. Though this is somewhat elevated when compared to the previous year's quarter of 0.95x leverage, I believe Diamondback is well-positioned to pay down debt through free cash flow generation.

Risks Related to Diamondback Energy

Bull Case

Diamondback may realize growth as a result of the increased interest in utilizing natural gas to support the increasing power capacity needs for the growing footprint of large-scale data centers. This can both directly and indirectly impact Diamondback in that the firm may directly sell gas to in-basin, gas-fired power plants, or realize strong selling prices from gas through total demand growth.

Bear Case

Oil remains pressured as a result of global market risk and the return of volumes supplied by OPEC+ beginning on April 1, 2025. This can potentially result in increased volumes of oil hitting the market while demand remains stagnant or declines.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Diamondback increased its dividend rate by 11% to an annualized rate of $4/share. With the higher base dividend rate, Diamondback has the potential to continue growing its total dividend payout through its added variable dividend component. The firm also repurchased 2.3mm shares for $402mm in Q4 2024. Total shares repurchased in FY 2024 summed to 5.5mm for a value of $959mm.

Given the upside potential, I am recommending FANG shares with a BUY rating and a price target of $238/share at 7.66x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm's "financial position" section and ties it to the stock's historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock's peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company's stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Biggest Jumps In Dividend Yields
data.description:
While the tariff situation has created a great deal of uncertainty and stock prices have crashed in tow, one silver lining is that dividend yields have at least ticked up. Currently, the S&P 500's dividend yield of 1.64% is the highest it has been since November 2023. For the average member that pays a dividend, it has seen a 33 bps increase in its yield, up to 2.58%.

Additionally, there are 59 S&P 500 members that now have a higher yield than the 10-year Treasury. Of course, the impact of tariffs could materially impact earnings and hence their ability to pay a dividend at all, but holding that conversation aside, below we show the S&P 500 members that have seen the largest increases in their dividend yields since the sell-off began on February 19.

Of all members of the index, there are 34 to have seen a full percentage point increase in their yields as a result of the declines since the S&P's high. The largest increase has come from Dow (DOW) which now yields over 10% after falling over 30% since 2/19. That is also the single highest yield of all S&P 500 members. Of the rest of the list below, there are another five stocks that now rank in the top ten highest yields: LyondellBassell (LYB), Pfizer (PFE), Franklin Resources (BEN), APA Corp (APA), and United Parcel Services (UPS).

Original Post

data.title: Mercury Systems: Significant Earnings Improvement But Lacks Near-Term Upside
data.description:
I have been covering Mercury Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:MRCY) since May 2023 and while I initially had a buy rating on the stock, I had to downgrade the stock to hold. The reason was that the company had a relatively high share of development programs in its revenue mix. These programs have lower margins and the company spent much of FY24 de-risking. That is a fancy way to say that the company was experiencing cost overruns as its contracts left inflationary cost pressures and development risk to be carried by the contractor.

This year should be a lot better with the first big steps in margin recovery and reverting to a healthier mix between development revenues and regular product revenues. In November, I reiterated my hold rating as there was no upside in the near term.

The stock lost 5.4% since my last coverage compared to a 15.7% decline for the stock market. In this report, I will discuss the most recent earnings and assess whether the improvement in earnings and the lower share price provide a more compelling investment case.

Mercury Systems Maintains Margin Expansion Momentum

In the second quarter, bookings decreased to $242.4 million. That is not a major concern as the company’s backlog has increased 6% to $1.35 billion with stable visibility on sales in the next 12 months.

Revenues increased 13% driven by some $30 million in sales being achieved in Q2 instead of Q3. Operating expenses declined by 14% as the company streamlined its SG&A and R&D expenses as part of a restructuring announced in January 2024. We are seeing the results of that now more prominently. The company remained in a GAAP net loss position, but the loss tapered significantly, and adjusted EBITDA swung from a $21.3 million loss to a $22 million profit.

Operating cash flow surged to $81.9 million driven by an acceleration of cash collections from Q3 into Q2. This resulted in free cash flow growth from $37.5 million to $81.9 million. That growth will not be maintained as the second half is expected to be flat in terms of free cash flow generation.

So, the H1 free cash flow of $61 million is also what the company expects to book in free cash flow for the full year. However, overall, we are seeing significantly better margins due to the nature of the work as well as volumes, and we are seeing better free cash flow.

The company is definitely not where it is supposed to be as EBITDA margins should double, but the progress is promising.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Mercury Systems?

My risk assessment for Mercury Systems is largely unchanged. Currently, there is some headwind from the mix with more development contracts blended in. These contracts tend to have lower margins. The risk for Mercury Systems and other defense contractors has been that it was locked in contracts on which costs rose and there was no pass-through mechanism.

The company has largely worked through those risks and is now seeing a higher margin backlog with a work in progress on bringing the development mix back towards around 20% of revenues. That provides a significant improvement in performance year-on-year and as higher margins programs also ramp up that should provide additional upside to earnings.

There is some concern regarding defense budgets contracting and that can put pressure on the industry as a whole. However, I do believe that microelectronics as developed and manufactured by Mercury Systems are becoming increasingly more important in a modern battlefield.

The company earlier expected sales to be roughly flat but with a significant uptick in cash flow generation and EBITDA. It is now expecting mid-single digit growth.

Mercury Systems Upsides Remains Towards 2027

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I previously pointed out that the most upside for Mercury Systems is more towards FY27 than in the near term and that observation remains. However, my FY26 price target has increased from $39.23 to $45.04 providing an 8% upside. That is not enough to justify a buy rating at this moment, but we are seeing some very positive developments.

EBITDA estimates for FY25 have increased by 4.3% and in FY26 and FY27 the estimates have now increased by 7.2% and 11.6%. So, the company finally seems to be on a stronger growth path and has exceeded analyst estimates which have resulted in upward revisions for the years ahead. Free cash flow is also expected to be significantly better this year and that also sparked higher expectations for next year.

I do believe that the company has to refinance debt to cover the maturing $591.5 million revolver in FY27. That debt is the only bank debt the company has, and I do believe that the company should be able to either refinance that maturing debt or expand the term for the revolved as its earnings have substantially improved.

So, I do see a lot of improvement but still no justification for valuing the stock in line with its median elevated EV/EBITDA. A peer group valuation shows increased appeal in FY27 and with that in mind, I am maintaining my hold rating but with a higher price target.

Conclusion: Mercury Systems Inches Closer To A Buy Rating

Mercury Systems is seeing significant improvements in its financial performance. The company did have some tailwinds in Q2 from revenues and cash collection being pulled forward. However, absent of that transient effect the company lowered its cost through restructuring and is improving its margin mix.

The company is not yet at the point where a buy rating is justified, but it is inching closer to that point.

data.title: NatWest: 7% Forward Dividend Yield Makes It An Attractive Income Play (Upgrade)
data.description:
As I’ve covered in previous articles, I was not particularly bullish on NatWest Group Plc (NYSE:NWG) in recent months, but its share price performance has been quite strong over the last year, supported by the U.K. government stake selldown and higher capital returns to shareholders.

Since my last article on NatWest, the U.K. government has been selling down its stake and currently holds less than 4% of the bank’s capital, being on the right path to return NatWest to full private ownership during this year or 2026. While recent market volatility may lead to a longer time frame for the U.K. government to fully exit the bank’s capital, this is not expected to impact much of its strategy. Moreover, due to its size and the relatively concentrated banking market in the U.K., I don’t expect NatWest to be part of significant M&A activity in the near future, thus, while returning to full private ownership is an important milestone for the bank, it’s not expected to change much its business outlook ahead.

Since my last article on NatWest, the bank has also released its 2024 earnings, which were resilient and above street estimates. Despite that, its growth was relatively low as the Bank of England started a rate-cut cycle last July, cutting its official bank rate from 5.25%, to 4.5% nowadays, being a headwind for top-line growth.

While lower interest rates are naturally negative for bank revenues, this was not an unexpected move, and NatWest, like its peers, had plenty of time to adapt its balance sheet to lower rates and reduce interest rate sensitivity.

Indeed, NatWest’s net interest margin (NIM) improved by 1 basis point during 2024 to 2.13%, supported by lower funding costs and a slight decline in deposit costs, which were more than enough to offset declining lending margins during last year.

Lower lending margins were justified by strong competition in the banking market, especially related to mortgages, which is a key banking product for NatWest and its closest competitors Lloyds Banking Group (LYG) and Nationwide. Despite that, the bank was able to grow its mortgage book by acquiring some £2.5 billion of prime mortgages from Metro Bank, as NatWest sees mortgages as a key banking product to grow its loan book, while organically it was losing some market share to competitors. Indeed, despite this acquisition, its mortgage book only increased by £1.4 billion in 2024, as mortgage amortization was higher than new mortgage production during the year.

Nevertheless, NatWest’s loan book increased by 3.5% YoY to £372 billion at the end of 2024, as the bank was able to grow loans in other segments, including credit cards, commercial lending, and corporate loans. This was also an important contribution to offset lower interest rates in the second half of 2024, as higher volumes helped to offset declining market rates.

On the liabilities side, the bank was able to increase deposits by £12.2 billion, which was almost the same as loan growth (£12.7 billion) during 2024. Therefore, its loan-to-deposits ratio remained at a conservative level (86% at the end of 2024, while usually a ratio below 100% is considered acceptable), showing that NatWest was able to finance its loan book growth through retail and corporate deposits and is not much dependent on wholesale financing, which is a positive factor for the sustainability of its business model over the long term.

Another positive factor for its net interest income resilience in 2024 was the bank’s structural hedge, which generated more £200 million of income last year compared to 2023 as the bank reinvested securities at higher rates than compared to the ones that matured. Indeed, the nominal amount of the hedge portfolio declined to £172 billion (vs. £185 billion in 2023), but the average yield increased to 1.75% (vs. 1.42% in 2023). This happens because securities reaching maturity had yields closer to zero, while the bank has reinvested proceeds to yield around 3.5%. This leads to higher income from the structural hedge portfolio, being a good contributor for NII resilience.

Given this backdrop of stable NIM, a higher loan book, and increasing gross income from the structural hedge portfolio, it’s not surprising to see that NatWest’s NII increased by 2.2% YoY to £11.3 billion in 2024, excluding one-off effects. Its other revenues also performed well, increasing by 2.6% YoY to £3.34 billion in the year, also excluding notable items. However, when including one-off items, such as forex gains and losses, NatWest’s revenues amounted to £14.7 billion in 2024, representing a decline of 0.8% compared to the previous year.

This was not unexpected to me, as I’ve covered in previous articles, as I was expecting some top-line weakness in the short term as the bank’s NII is naturally impacted by lower market rates despite its efforts to reduce interest rate sensitivity. On the other hand, inflation has remained sticky, and interest rate volatility has been relatively high recently, with the street expecting now fewer rate cuts than compared to some months ago.

This is positive for a more resilient NII ahead, plus the bank is also expecting to maintain some loan growth during 2025 and additionally, its structural hedge portfolio will also contribute to higher income over the coming years. As shown in the next graph, NatWest expects gross income from the structural hedge to increase every year until 2027, with the bank expecting gross income to grow by about £1 billion in 2025 compared to the previous year.

These are positive tailwinds for revenue growth in the short to medium term that NatWest expects to be enough to offset potential lower rates over the coming quarters. For 2025, its guidance is to achieve total revenues between £15.2 and 15.7 billion (excluding notable items), representing annual growth between 3-7%, which is relatively good considering the challenging operating backdrop.

Regarding costs, the bank reported total expenses of £8.15 billion in 2024 (+1.9% YoY), which can be considered a positive development given the inflationary environment and upward pressure on wage costs and other administrative expenses. NatWest has been investing in digitalization and other technology developments to improve efficiency, which has been a key driver to offset cost pressure in recent years. Its cost-to-income ratio was 55% in 2024, which is not among the best efficiency ratios in the European banking sector, but it’s still an acceptable level compared to other retail-oriented banks.

Regarding credit quality, it remained quite good during 2024 and was another positive factor for earnings growth, as the cost of risk ratio was only 9 bps (vs. 15 bps in 2023). This resulted in loan loss charges of £359 million in the year, representing an annual decline of more than £200 million. This good credit quality is explained by the bank’s conservative approach to credit risk, being largely exposed to prime mortgages with low loan-to-value ratios and managed exposure to sectors at risk. Going forward, the bank is not expected to change much its credit underwriting standards, expecting its cost of risk ratio to remain below 20 bps over the medium term.

Due to the combination of slightly lower revenues, higher expenses, and lower credit costs, its net income was slightly above £4.5 billion in 2024, up by 2.8% YoY. Due to higher capital, its return on tangible equity (RoTE) ratio declined 30 bps in the year, to 17.5%. Nevertheless, this is a very good level of profitability in the banking sector, showing that NatWest’s business model is strong as the bank has a relatively low-risk profile and good profitability.

Moreover, its organic capital generation is also quite good, allowing the bank to distribute a good part of its earnings to shareholders. However, its capital return policy has been somewhat conservative given that NatWest’s dividend payout ratio target was only 40%, returning further excess capital through share buybacks.

During 2024, the bank returned some £4 billion to shareholders both through dividends and directed share buybacks (buying the government’s stake), representing a total payout ratio of about 89%. This shows that NatWest is comfortable with its capital position and does not need to retain many earnings, a profile that is not expected to change much in the near future.

Indeed, its CET1 ratio was 13.6% at the end of 2024 (+20 bps compared to 2023), being within its target of 13-14% over the medium term, which is an acceptable capital ratio compared to other European banks, even though it’s below the sector’s average that is currently closer to 15%. However, as the bank has a low-risk profile and is heavily exposed to mortgages in the U.K., I don’t think its below-average capital ratio is concerning, and the bank seems to be in a good position to distribute most of its earnings to shareholders over the coming years.

The bank is also positive about its business outlook, expecting to achieve mid-single-digit earnings growth over the next three years and maintain a RoTE ratio above 15%. Regarding its dividend, it has raised its payout ratio to 50% in 2025 and thereafter, while further excess capital should be returned through share buybacks.

Its annual dividend was £0.215 per share in 2024, representing an increase of 26% from the previous year, leading to a dividend yield slightly above 5% at its current share price. As the bank is expected to perform share buybacks this year and will increase the dividend payout ratio by 10 percentage points related to 2025 earnings, its dividend per share is expected to increase to £0.279 (+29% YoY). Considering this future dividend, its forward dividend yield increases to around 7%, making it quite attractive for income-oriented investors.

Regarding its valuation, NatWest is currently trading near book value, following some share price weakness in recent weeks due to Trump’s tariff drama. The bank was trading near 1.15x book value about one month ago; thus, the recent market selloff had a significant impact on NatWest’s valuation, creating a buying opportunity for long-term investors, in my opinion. Compared to its closest peer, Lloyds, NatWest is trading at a very similar multiple, but its profitability has been higher in recent years, being a sign that NatWest offers more value nowadays than its peer.

Conclusion

NatWest has reported a solid operating performance during 2024, showing that its business model is highly profitable and generates significant capital. While its growth prospects aren’t impressive, the bank should deliver some dividend and earnings growth over the coming years, and doesn’t need to retain many earnings due to a solid capital position. If NatWest delivers on its dividend guidance for 2025, it’s now offering a forward dividend yield of about 7%, making it a good income play in the European banking sector.
